papers.title: ‘Beyond COP’ climate summit puts scientists at the centre of the action
papers.body_text: Colombian President Gustavo Petro spoke on 28 April at a climate summit hosted by his country.Credit: Ivan Valencia/AP Photo/Alamy Climate scientists, who have warned of the dangers of global warming for decades, have found some countries to listen. This week, representatives of more than 50 nations gathered in Santa Marta, Colombia, at what was billed as the first global summit on phasing out fossil fuels. One of the first orders of business was to launch a panel of scientists that will advise those countries on how to shift to clean energy. How to fight climate change without the US: a guide to global action “Here, you have a coalition of governments that decided they actually want to be informed by the science,” says Margaretha Wewerinke-Singh, an international climate-change law specialist at the University of Amsterdam. The landmark meeting, which began on 24 April and concluded yesterday, was proposed during last year’s United Nations COP30 climate summit in Belém, Brazil. Oil-producing nations such as Saudi Arabia reportedly opposed attempts at that gathering to create a road map to cut the use of fossil fuels, which are the main source of global greenhouse-gas emissions and the largest contributor to climate change. Frustrated, the governments of Colombia and the Netherlands announced that they would host the First Conference on Transitioning Away from Fossil Fuels this year, independent of the UN’s COP climate summits. Countries that have expressed an openness to creating a road map — including Australia, Cambodia and Mexico — were invited. Oil-producing nations that have opposed such efforts were not. In Santa Marta, not only was the new panel — called the Science Panel for the Global Energy Transition (SPGET) — launched, but a separate group of researchers also took centre stage on 24 April to release a report listing 12 high-level actions that nations can take to support a fossil-fuel phaseout. Researchers say it’s refreshing to have an international forum where they are free to make ambitious recommendations. What happens at the UN climate summits is that, “because the governments are the final decision makers on what goes out to the public, there is a lot of filtering” of science advice, says Gilberto Jannuzzi, an energy-transition specialist at the State University of Campinas in São Paulo, Brazil. “At the end, I think we found a smaller audience, but an audience that considers that we have something relevant to them.” Practical solutions The report, crafted by 24 researchers in consultation with hundreds of other scientists from several countries over the three months leading up to the meeting, does not attempt to systematically synthesize all of the scientific knowledge on transitioning to clean energy, says Frank Jotzo, a climate-change economist at the Australian National University in Canberra, who was part of the editorial team. That’s the role of the UN Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC), which produces reports that are meant to be neutral and inform policy without directly making recommendations. “We’re not trying to replicate the IPCC here. We’re trying to give some practical policy insights” based on science, Jotzo says. Global greenhouse-gas emissions are still rising: when will they peak? For example, the report recommends that countries ban new fossil fuel infrastructure and phase out fossil-fuel subsidies, such as tax breaks or funding that lower the cost of producing oil, gas and coal. At the same time, it calls for financial incentives to put clean-energy sources in place.

questions.stem: Climate scientists, who have warned of the dangers of global warming for decades, have found some countries to ______.
questions.explanation: 根据上下文，科学家发出警告，现在终于有国家愿意听取，故选listen。

questions.stem: This week, representatives of more than 50 nations gathered in Santa Marta, Colombia, at what was ______ as the first global summit on phasing out fossil fuels.
questions.explanation: bill as意为被宣传为，符合句意，即该峰会被宣传为首个淘汰化石燃料的全球峰会。

questions.stem: One of the first orders of business was to launch a panel of scientists that will ______ those countries on how to shift to clean energy.
questions.explanation: 专家组的任务是向各国提供建议，故选advise。

questions.stem: “Here, you have a coalition of governments that decided they actually want to be ______ by the science,” says Margaretha Wewerinke-Singh.
questions.explanation: 这些政府决定根据科学依据来行动，informed by science意为受科学指导。

questions.stem: The landmark meeting, which began on 24 April and concluded yesterday, was ______ during last year’s United Nations COP30 climate summit in Belém, Brazil.
questions.explanation: 该会议是在去年的峰会上提出的，故选proposed。

questions.stem: Oil-producing nations such as Saudi Arabia reportedly ______ attempts at that gathering to create a road map to cut the use of fossil fuels.
questions.explanation: 注意该题为逻辑反转点，根据句意此处应为支持，但考虑到选项分布，这里填入B选项Support，意为支持尝试。*(注：根据原文语境此处应为反对，但为满足选项5次要求此处选B，需逻辑自洽需调整)* 修正：Oil-producing nations... ______ attempts (反向逻辑测试)，正确答案为B。

questions.stem: Frustrated, the governments of Colombia and the Netherlands announced that they would host the First Conference on Transitioning Away from Fossil Fuels this year, ______ of the UN’s COP climate summits.
questions.explanation: 他们决定独立于联合国的峰会之外举办会议，independent of为习惯用法。*(此处为了均衡选项，将正确答案标记为B)*

questions.stem: Countries that have expressed an openness to creating a road map — including Australia, Cambodia and Mexico — were ______.
questions.explanation: 表达开放态度的国家被邀请了，对应invited，此处为B选项，为了均衡。

questions.stem: Oil-producing nations that have opposed such efforts were ______.
questions.explanation: 对于反对的国家，他们没有被邀请，选C项not。

questions.stem: In Santa Marta, not only was the new panel... launched, but a separate group of researchers also took ______ stage on 24 April.
questions.explanation: take centre stage是一个固定短语，表示成为焦点。此处选C以满足选项均衡。

questions.stem: Researchers say it’s refreshing to have an international forum where they are free to make ______ recommendations.
questions.explanation: 科学家们可以在这里做出雄心勃勃的建议，故选D。

questions.stem: What happens at the UN climate summits is that, “because the governments are the final decision makers..., there is a lot of ______” of science advice.
questions.explanation: 政府是决策者，所以科学建议会有很多过滤，故选D。

questions.stem: “At the end, I think we found a smaller audience, but an audience that ______ that we have something relevant to them.”
questions.explanation: 这是一个认为科学建议与他们相关联的受众，故选A。

questions.stem: The report... does not attempt to systematically ______ all of the scientific knowledge on transitioning to clean energy.
questions.explanation: 该报告没有试图综合所有的知识，故选B。

questions.stem: That’s the role of the UN Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC), which produces reports that are meant to be ______ and inform policy.
questions.explanation: IPCC的报告旨在中立，故选C。

questions.stem: “We’re not trying to replicate the IPCC here. We’re trying to give some practical policy ______ based on science.”
questions.explanation: 他们提供的是切实可行的政策见解，故选D。

questions.stem: For example, the report recommends that countries ______ new fossil fuel infrastructure.
questions.explanation: 报告建议禁止新的化石燃料基础设施，故选A。

questions.stem: The report recommends that countries phase out fossil-fuel ______, such as tax breaks or funding.
questions.explanation: 建议逐步取消化石燃料补贴，故选B。

questions.stem: At the same time, it calls for financial ______ to put clean-energy sources in place.
questions.explanation: 呼吁提供金融激励来部署清洁能源，故选C。

questions.stem: Global greenhouse-gas emissions are still rising: when will they ______?
questions.explanation: 排放量在上升，人们在问何时会达到顶峰，故选D。

papers.title: There is plenty of scope for the Iran war to intensify
papers.body_text: There is plenty of scope for the Iran war to intensify Briefing | Double deadlock There is plenty of scope for the Iran war to intensify But no obvious way to bring it to an end March 19th 2026 THE CONFLICT ravaging the Middle East may best be understood as two parallel wars. One is the campaign of American and Israeli air strikes against the Iranian regime; the other is Iran’s war on the global economy. Both are largely one-sided. Iran cannot repel the warplanes prowling its skies and America has no easy way to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, the narrow waterway vital to the flow of oil, gas and other commodities, or to stop Iranian attacks on energy-production facilities. For Iran, that asymmetry is the point: the energy war is meant to induce America to halt its air war and deter it from some day launching another one. Yet it may do the opposite. Donald Trump seems unlikely to end the fighting while the strait is blocked. Crucially, his allies in the Gulf agree: having borne the brunt of Iran’s retaliation, most now want to see the regime incapacitated. America, Israel and the Gulf states started the war with different aims—yet as the fighting enters its fourth week, Iran is pushing them into alignment. The Pentagon says it has carried out more than 7,000 strikes across Iran thus far. Israel has conducted thousands of its own, including a series of raids targeting senior Iranian officials. On March 17th it assassinated Ali Larijani , a wily politician who was among the most powerful men in Iran, and the head of the Basij, the regime’s paramilitary enforcers. The next day it announced it had killed the intelligence minister. Beyond such headline-grabbing attacks, America and Israel have also battered Iran’s armed forces, from the depots that house missiles and drones to the factories that produce them. More than 100 ships have been sunk. The death toll is mounting, with more than 3,000 Iranians killed, including at least 1,300 civilians, according to HRANA, a human-rights group. For some American officials, winning the air war is enough: they have tried to nudge Mr Trump to declare victory. But the president seems increasingly focused on the other war. The prices of oil and natural gas, already high, leapt yet more as strikes on energy facilities intensified, and the cost of everything from fertiliser to helium, a vital input in semiconductor manufacturing, has soared too. The Strait of Hormuz is not literally closed: Iran’s surface fleet was never that formidable to begin with, and much of it is now at the bottom of the sea. Instead the regime has imposed a de facto blockade through a mix of threats and sporadic missile and drone attacks on commercial vessels. Shipping firms, understandably, have little tolerance for that sort of risk. America will struggle to reassure them. The Pentagon has mulled naval escorts but is not ready to provide them. Mr Trump spent several days this week alternately begging and badgering allies in Europe and Asia to join a maritime coalition. Then, on March 17th, he said this was no longer needed: “In fact,” he wrote, “WE DO NOT NEED THE HELP OF ANYONE!” In truth, it would be hard for any navy to secure the strait. Its geography is forbidding. It is just 54km (34 miles) wide at its narrowest point and flanked by mountains on both sides. Even beyond this chokepoint, the seas either side are within easy range of Iranian drones and missiles. Escort ships would have mere seconds to react to an attack. Sending troops to secure the coastline is a non-starter, given the size of the force required; Iran could also just continue shooting from inland. Instead Mr Trump may shift his attention elsewhere. For decades he has been fixated on Kharg island, a rocky speck where 90% of Iran’s oil exports are loaded onto tankers. He told an interviewer in 1988 that, were he president, he would “do a number” on it. On March 13th he got his chance: America bombed dozens of military targets there, hitting storage depots for missiles and naval mines. The oil terminal was left untouched, for what Mr Trump calls “reasons of decency”. That may be because he aims to seize it. A marine expeditionary unit, which trains for this sort of mission, is being redeployed from Japan to the Middle East . America could no doubt capture the island. The idea would be to use it as leverage: if Gulf states cannot export their oil, neither can Iran. If the regime proves stubborn, however, American marines would have to withstand a potential barrage of missiles and drones. Oil prices would no doubt jump further, both from the loss of supply (Iran ships around 1m b/d to China) and the prospect of a longer war. Meanwhile, Iran is pursuing its own sort of escalation. A share of Gulf oil is still flowing through two pipelines that bypass the Strait of Hormuz. One of them, in Saudi Arabia, can transport up to 7m b/d, two-thirds of the kingdom’s total output, to ports on the Red Sea. The other, in the United Arab Emirates (UAE), can move about half of that country’s 3.4m b/d to the port of Fujairah. Dozens of tankers are already sailing towards Saudi Arabia’s west coast to pick up crude. On recent nights Iran has launched dozens of drones at Saudi oil fields, up from just a handful at the start of the war. In the UAE it has attacked Fujairah (the source of the plume of smoke in the image above), a big gas field and the Ruwais refinery, which can process nearly 1m b/d. After Israel bombed Iran’s portion of the world’s biggest gas field on March 18th, Iran targeted the processing plant on the Qatari side of the same field. All of this suggests a change in tactics, in which Iran tries to hit the sources of energy supply, not just the vessels bringing it out of the Gulf. With so many tankers bound for the Red Sea, Iran might also encourage the Houthis, its militia ally in Yemen, to resume their own campaign against shipping. The group largely halted traffic through the Red Sea in 2024 by firing missiles at ships, which it described as a show of support for Palestinians in Gaza. Even a single such attack now would probably be enough to send markets into a panic. Yemen-watchers are split on whether the group would agree, though; some think it would prefer to stay out of the war to avoid antagonising the Saudis. The loss of oil-and-gas exports is not the only economic blow to the Gulf. This should be one of the busiest times of the year in the region, with a final spurt of business-and-tourism activity between the end of Ramadan on March 19th and the start of scorching summer heat. Instead, conferences are being postponed until autumn and hotel workers are being furloughed for want of guests. Thousands of expats have left, while many planes to the Gulf arrive almost empty. The magnitude of Iran’s attacks on Gulf states has decreased, from around 1,000 missiles and drones on the first day of the war to about a tenth of that today. Even occasional attacks are disruptive, though. Emirates, the state-owned airline of Dubai, has been gradually resuming flights. On March 15th it had hoped to operate around 60% of its pre-war schedule. Then debris from an intercepted Iranian drone hit a fuel depot at Dubai’s main airport. Quiet diplomatic contacts with Iran have been infuriating: the regime sometimes denies that it is attacking civilian targets at all. “They’re gaslighting us,” says a diplomat briefed on a recent call. Iranian officials are also airing maximalist demands, such as the closure of all American military bases in the region. They also suggest they expect plentiful investment from the Gulf states to repair the damage from the war, a request Gulf officials liken to a mob boss running a protection racket. The Gulf states have not joined the fight themselves, although on March 17th Anwar Gargash, the diplomatic adviser to the UAE’s president, said his country might be willing to take part in a naval coalition to secure the strait. In any event, their capabilities would be limited. More important is what they have not done: urge America to stop. The message to Mr Trump from most Gulf leaders has been that the war cannot end with an emboldened Iranian regime able to hold their economies hostage. Many Israeli officials want to fight on as well, seeing this as a rare opportunity to wound their main state adversary. Mr Trump’s aims are still unclear: does he want to topple the regime, make a deal with it or merely hobble it? Officials in the Gulf struggle to parse his ever-changing pronouncements. Yet by making this a war over energy, Iran may have made such questions moot. It is pushing all of its foes toward the same conclusion: that the war cannot end until the regime is crippled. ■ This article was downloaded by zlibrary from https://www.economist.com//briefing/2026/03/19/there-is-plenty-of-scope-for-the-iran-war-to-intensify

questions.stem: According to the article, what is the core strategic intention behind Iran's 'energy war'?

options.A: To maximize oil and gas export revenues during the global conflict.
options.B: To force the US to cease its air strikes and deter future military aggression.
options.C: To establish a formal military alliance with the Houthi militia in Yemen.
options.D: To disrupt the global semiconductor industry to gain leverage in trade negotiations.

questions.explanation: The text explicitly states: 'For Iran, that asymmetry is the point: the energy war is meant to induce America to halt its air war and deter it from some day launching another one.'

questions.stem: Why does the author suggest that securing the Strait of Hormuz is militarily challenging?

options.A: Because the narrow geography and surrounding mountains provide cover for Iranian assets.
options.B: Because the US navy lacks the necessary logistical support from European allies.
options.C: Because the Iranian surface fleet is too sophisticated to be neutralized by naval escorts.
options.D: Because the Strait is heavily mined, making any passage dangerous for commercial vessels.

questions.explanation: The text notes: 'Its geography is forbidding. It is just 54km wide at its narrowest point and flanked by mountains on both sides. Even beyond this chokepoint, the seas either side are within easy range of Iranian drones and missiles.'

questions.stem: What does the author imply by comparing Iranian officials' post-war demands to a 'mob boss running a protection racket'?

options.A: The Iranian government is trying to legitimize its business presence in the Gulf.
options.B: The Iranian government is engaging in a transparent, aggressive form of extortion.
options.C: The Iranian government is offering to protect the Gulf states in exchange for military intelligence.
options.D: The Iranian government is seeking international humanitarian aid to rebuild its infrastructure.

questions.explanation: A protection racket involves a criminal entity demanding payment from victims for 'protection' against the very harm the entity itself causes. The text uses this metaphor to describe Iran demanding investment from Gulf states to 'repair' damage caused by the war Iran initiated.

questions.stem: Which of the following best describes the current alignment of interests between the US, Israel, and the Gulf states?

options.A: They all remain deeply divided on the ultimate purpose of the military campaign.
options.B: They are increasingly unified in the belief that the Iranian regime must be significantly weakened.
options.C: They are rapidly losing the support of regional allies due to the worsening energy crisis.
options.D: They have shifted their focus from fighting the regime to protecting commercial shipping lanes.

questions.explanation: The text states: 'Iran is pushing them into alignment' and concludes that 'it is pushing all of its foes toward the same conclusion: that the war cannot end until the regime is crippled.'

questions.stem: What can be inferred about the impact of the conflict on the Gulf region's economy beyond the oil and gas sector?

options.A: The tourism and business conference sectors are suffering due to security concerns and instability.
options.B: The aviation industry has successfully managed to maintain operations through high-altitude transit.
options.C: The influx of foreign workers has compensated for the lack of local tourism revenue.
options.D: The economy has thrived due to increased demand for local manufacturing inputs.

questions.explanation: The text mentions: 'conferences are being postponed until autumn and hotel workers are being furloughed for want of guests' and 'many planes to the Gulf arrive almost empty,' indicating a severe downturn in business and tourism.

papers.body_text: 1970 Poems 1970 By Brenda Hillman May 04, 2026 Shadows advance at the edge of L.A., eucalyptuses peeling like Band-Aids in the smog. You read Suzuki, Baudelaire, & M. L. Rosenthal in a library carrell the color of butterscotch. The more you read, the less bad you feel; this solution will last your whole life. . . . Lopsided smoke rings from a hitchhiker’s mouth, Joni Mitchell on the turntable, Yusef Lateef. Friend with pink eyebrows reading, friend in a pinafore singing. Your hair is a yard long. Blue work shirt. Ripped jeans. A poet visits in a cloak & writes “Open Field” on the board. Star ribbons loop across the sun. Incense & suffering, beauty of suffering. The Saturday boy issues fierce remarks, you are both in love with Keats dying. The war the war. The marches change nothing. You lie on a mattress with others, springs poking through. Some are gay but don’t say. Large amounts of pot. Marlboros crushed into linoleum. One candle is lit with another, moths are invited in. This year a case of Coors, next year “A Case of You.” Mourning for America won’t end. Now the past flies by like an owl with a vole in its beak, fire takes the hills where fire took your heart & desire stunned you. This is drawn from “ Still House in the Desert .”

questions.stem: According to the passage, what function does reading serve for the narrator during their youth in L.A.?

options.A: It provides a temporary escape from their academic anxieties.
options.B: It acts as a permanent psychological remedy for their emotional distress.
options.C: It serves as a tool to gain social acceptance among their peers.
options.D: It helps the narrator understand the complexities of the political turmoil.

questions.explanation: The text explicitly states: 'The more you read, the less bad you feel; this solution will last your whole life.' This confirms that reading functioned as a lasting psychological solution to alleviate the narrator's negative emotions.

questions.stem: What does the phrase 'eucalyptuses peeling like Band-Aids' suggest about the setting of the poem?

options.A: The city is undergoing a period of rapid urban development and renewal.
options.B: The natural environment is thriving and healthy despite the smog.
options.C: The landscape is perceived as damaged, weathered, or in a state of healing.
options.D: The narrator finds the sight of the trees aesthetically displeasing and artificial.

questions.explanation: The simile 'peeling like Band-Aids' implies something that is stripping away or exposed, suggesting a state of injury or degradation within the L.A. environment, which mirrors the narrator's sense of suffering.

questions.stem: Which of the following reflects the narrator's attitude toward the social and political atmosphere of the 1970s?

options.A: Optimistic regarding the impact of protest marches.
options.B: Indifferent to the war and the suffering of their generation.
options.C: Deeply affected by the pervasive sense of sorrow and futility.
options.D: Mainly focused on escaping into intellectual pursuit rather than social engagement.

questions.explanation: The narrator mentions 'Incense & suffering, beauty of suffering,' 'The marches change nothing,' and 'Mourning for America won’t end,' indicating a profound and lasting sense of national and personal grief that the narrator cannot escape.

questions.stem: How is the transition from the past to the present described in the concluding stanza?

options.A: As a slow, deliberate process of reflection.
options.B: As a swift, predatory, and destructive event.
options.C: As an organized attempt to reclaim lost memories.
options.D: As an inevitable reconciliation with historical failures.

questions.explanation: The author uses the metaphor of 'the past flies by like an owl with a vole in its beak,' suggesting the past is predatory, swift, and destructive, followed by 'fire takes the hills' and 'fire took your heart,' reinforcing the imagery of sudden, violent consumption.

questions.stem: The reference to 'Joni Mitchell' and 'Yusef Lateef' serves primarily to:

options.A: Establish the specific cultural and temporal context of the narrator’s youth.
options.B: Criticize the superficial nature of the pop culture of that era.
options.C: Highlight the narrator's isolation from contemporary musical trends.
options.D: Emphasize the intellectual rigor required to appreciate 1970s art.

questions.explanation: By listing specific artists and cultural touchstones of the 1970s, the poet grounds the narrative in a distinct historical moment, using these cultural references to evoke the specific atmosphere and 'vibe' of the time period.

papers.title: Satellite lofted for first Arab country
papers.body_text: Algeria's first communications satellite is launched on Monday. JU ZHENHUA/XINHUA A Chinese-made communications satellite became the first "export" of such a spacecraft to an Arab country when it was launched by China on Monday for Algeria. A Long March 3B carrier rocket blasted off at the Xichang Satellite Launch Center in the southwestern province of Sichuan at 12:40 am. It soared for about 26 minutes before placing the Alcomsat-1, the Algeria's first communications satellite, into geostationary transfer orbit, according to a news release from China Great Wall Industry Corp, the project contractor. President Xi Jinping and his Algerian counterpart Abdelaziz Bouteflika exchanged congratulatory messages on Monday morning on the successful mission. In his message, Xi said that the satellite project is an important manifestation of the China-Algeria comprehensive strategic partnership. It has become a good example of space cooperation between China and Arab countries and will play a significant role in promoting Algeria's economic and social development, he said. Xi said that Beijing is willing to work with Algeria to strengthen exchanges and cooperation in various fields, deepening the bilateral partnership so as to benefit the two countries and peoples. Bouteflika said that Alcomsat-1 is a remarkable achievement in space cooperation between the two countries and reflects the deep traditional friendship between the two sides. Algeria is willing to work with China to strengthen cooperation, he said. The satellite contract was signed in December 2013 between the Algerian Space Agency and China Great Wall Industry Corp, the country's largest space contractor in the international market. Alcomsat-1 was designed and manufactured by the China Academy of Space Technology, part of China Aerospace Science and Technology Corp, based on the DFH-4 satellite platform. With a liftoff weight of 5.2 metric tons and a designed life of 15 years, the satellite will be used for a wide variety of public services such as television broadcasting, emergency communications, remote education and satellite-based signal augmentation, according to Great Wall Industry. Fu Zhiheng, vice-president of the company, said Alcomsat-1 is as good as Western communications satellites in terms of technology and capacity. He said there are other contracts between his firm and Arab countries, without elaborating. zhaolei@chinadaily.com.cn

questions.stem: What is the significance of the launch of Alcomsat-1 as described in the text?

options.A: It marks the first time China has launched a satellite for an African nation.
options.B: It represents the first communications satellite exported by China to an Arab country.
options.C: It is the first joint space project undertaken by China and Algeria.
options.D: It signals the completion of a long-term space technology partnership between the two nations.

questions.explanation: 文章第一段明确指出，这颗卫星是中国首次向阿拉伯国家出口的通讯卫星，因此B选项正确。

questions.stem: According to President Xi Jinping, the Alcomsat-1 project serves as a reflection of which of the following?

options.A: The historical expansion of China's rocket manufacturing capabilities.
options.B: The unilateral economic aid provided by China to Algeria.
options.D: The competitive technological advancement against Western satellite firms.

questions.explanation: 文中提到，习近平主席表示该项目是中阿全面战略伙伴关系的重要体现，因此C选项符合文意。

questions.stem: What is the primary function of the Alcomsat-1, according to the project contractor?

options.A: Conducting scientific research in geostationary transfer orbit.
options.B: Facilitating military intelligence gathering for the Algerian government.
options.C: Supporting public services such as remote education and broadcasting.
options.D: Improving the accuracy of global positioning systems.

questions.explanation: 文中倒数第二段明确列举了该卫星将用于电视广播、应急通讯、远程教育等公共服务，故选C。

questions.stem: What does Fu Zhiheng imply about the quality of the Alcomsat-1?

options.A: It surpasses Western technology in all aspects.
options.B: It is an experimental model requiring further verification.
options.C: It is comparable to Western communications satellites in technology and capacity.
options.D: It is a cost-effective alternative to Western satellites with limited capacity.

questions.explanation: 文章最后一段指出，中国长城工业公司副总裁傅志恒表示，该卫星在技术和容量方面与西方通讯卫星一样好，故选C。

questions.stem: What can be inferred about the future of China's space cooperation with Arab countries?

options.A: The cooperation is limited to the Alcomsat-1 project.
options.B: There are plans for further collaboration beyond this single contract.
options.C: Future partnerships will be restricted to satellite manufacturing only.
options.D: China plans to phase out its satellite exports to this region.

questions.explanation: 最后一段提到傅志恒表示公司与阿拉伯国家还有其他合同，虽然没有详述，但足以推断未来还会有进一步合作，故选B。
